summary: A collection of excerpts from Iris Murdoch's early, unpublished letters to French novelist Raymond Queneau. The letters reveal her deep insecurities about her writing, her struggles with depression and romantic relationships, her views on religion, and her distaste for the vanity of social gatherings and certain academic philosophers.

principles.statement: Love rarely exists without causing mutual pain.
principles.rationale: Because of the deep emotional bond, if one person in a loving relationship is hurt, both parties inevitably suffer.
principles.source_quote: How rarely it (love) occurs without hurt to one or both parties – or rather both, for if one is hurt, both are hurt.

principles.statement: Christian values have real moral worth, even if the religion's literal doctrines are unbelievable.
principles.rationale: She recognizes the ethical value and reality of Christian principles, but cannot accept its supernatural elements, viewing them as a fairy tale.
principles.source_quote: I often wish I could be Christian, there is such worth there and values which are real to one but the rest remains a fairy tale to me.

signature_quotes.text: There is something heavy in what I write…that makes me wonder if I shall ever write something good.
signature_quotes.context: Written in 1946 regarding the first draft of her novel, expressing her deep self-doubt.

signature_quotes.text: How rarely it (love) occurs without hurt to one or both parties – or rather both, for if one is hurt, both are hurt.
signature_quotes.context: Written in 1947 after seeing Raymond Queneau.

signature_quotes.text: I often wish I could be Christian, there is such worth there and values which are real to one but the rest remains a fairy tale to me.
signature_quotes.context: Expressing her views on religion in a 1946 letter.

signature_quotes.text: I hate giving parties…all one’s worst motives seem uppermost – a desire to impress, to show off one’s friends, to show off one’s friend to each other, to play the clever hostess…all is vanity.
signature_quotes.context: Written in 1948 about her distaste for social hosting.

signature_quotes.text: Imagining my life will begin when I chuck philosophy and just write novels and poetry. Philosophy takes all my lifeblood at present and I’m not brilliant at it.
signature_quotes.context: Written in 1948 about her career struggles and desire to focus on creative writing.

anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It brings out a person's worst motives, such as vanity, the desire to impress others, and the urge to show off friends.

anti_patterns.description: Striving to be superficially "lucid and smart" in philosophy.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: She felt alienated by the "bright young men" at Cambridge who prioritized being smart and lucid, contrasting it with her own self-described "obscurantist" approach, suggesting a preference for grappling with heavier, more complex truths.
